arctic.title: The Arctic: Our Planet's Canary
arctic.heroSubtitle: A data-driven story of sea-ice decline by Lukas Kreibig
arctic.heroLine1: The Arctic is Warming Four Times Faster Than the Global Average
arctic.heroLine2: In the Fast-Warming Arctic, Sea-Ice Melt Is Now a Constant Reality
arctic.heroLine3: Scientists forecast ice-free Arctic summers before 2050
arctic.heroLine4: The Arctic is the planet's canary.

loading.title: Preparing the map and satellite layers
loading.detail: Data, imagery, and the Arctic flyover are loaded before the story begins.

geographic.title: How the Ice is Vanishing
geographic.intro: In the Arctic, sea ice is not just scenery. For many places it is route, workplace, and risk. What changes when that season becomes shorter and harder to trust?

greenland.title: Greenland
greenland.description: The world's largest island is mostly ice sheet. Along the rocky coast, deep fjords cut inland and create sheltered places where people have lived with the Arctic sea for millennia.

uummannaqBay.title: Uummannaq Bay
uummannaqBay.description: 600 kilometers north of the Arctic Circle, this bay opens into one of Greenland's most dramatic fjord systems. In winter, the sea can become a connection.

uummannaqIsland.title: Uummannaq Island
uummannaqIsland.description: About 1,300 people live here beneath a distinctive heart-shaped mountain. In Uummannaq, ice is not scenery. It is mobility, timing, and safety.
uummannaqIsland.changing: The question is how that reliability is changing.

heartOfSeal.title: Heart of a Seal
heartOfSeal.description: Uummannaq takes its name from the mountain known as the Heart of a Seal. Beneath it, the town sits between harbor, fjord, and winter routes.

winterMonths.description: When the fjord freezes, the map changes. Routes that do not exist in summer become possible by sled, snowmobile, or on foot, but only while the ice can be trusted.

fishing.description: Fishing still shapes Uummannaq. But the story is not only climate loss: ice conditions, harbor access, markets, boats, and deliberate ice breakup all affect daily life.

voices.title: Voices from Uummannaq
voices.description: In the Life on Thin Ice study, residents describe how winter ice, travel, hunting, and fishing are changing. Their observations are where this measurement begins.
voices.learnMoreLink: Find out more about the study "Life on thin ice"
voices.learnMoreContent: The paper "Life on thin ice: Insights from Uummannaq, Greenland for connecting climate science with Arctic communities" connects fieldwork in Uummannaq Bay with a review of sea-ice research. Interviews and a focus group show why predictable winter ice matters for hunting, fishing, and travel, and why coastal ice thickness is underrepresented in mainstream research. Newer work on co-production adds an important caution: local ice change is not a single-cause story. Fishing, harbor access, deliberate ice breakup, modernization, and local knowledge all shape how ice is used and experienced.

quotes.iceDisappears:
When I was a child,
the ice was gone in June or July.
Now it is gone in April or May.
quotes.unstableClimate:
The most important change is the less stable weather.
The wind has become stronger
and harder to predict.
quotes.motorsledge:
Many hunters now prefer snowmobiles to dog sleds.
The winters are shorter,
and the catch has to move faster.
quotes.source: from the "Life on Thin Ice" study

measurement.title: How to measure ice?
measurement.intro: But how can a local observation be tested? The bridge is a Sentinel-2 pipeline: satellite images made comparable, scene by scene.
measurement.step1: First, here's the top-down view our satellite sees.
measurement.step2: Now the raw winter image. Scenes like this are the basis for the measurement.
measurement.step3: Since 2017, ESA's Sentinel programme has provided regular images. Thousands of scenes have to become one consistent record.
measurement.step4: A computer-vision pipeline reads each image. It masks clouds and land, then separates ice from open water.
measurement.step5: The overlay is not the conclusion. It is the audit trail between satellite image, model, and chart.
measurement.learnMoreLink: Learn More about the Computer Vision Pipeline
measurement.learnMoreTitle: Computer Vision Pipeline for Sea Ice Detection
measurement.learnMoreContent:
High-resolution Sentinel-2 images (all 13 bands) are streamed from AWS and pass through a four-stage segmentation pipeline:

• Black-border filter removes corrupt edge pixels.
• Cloud mask: a U-Net model (CloudSEN12+) flags thin clouds and shadows.
• Land mask: a static coastline raster excludes rock and tundra.
• Ice/Water mask: spectral indices separate
  – solid ice  NDSI > 0.52
  – light / wet ice 0.31–0.52
  – open water NDWI > 0.25.

Masks are merged into a colour overlay (yellow = solid ice, cyan = light ice, blue = water). Statistics for every tile are stored in a CSV file for later analysis.

visualProof.title: A Decade of Seasons
visualProof.description: Each small chart shows one Uummannaq ice season from February to June.
visualProof.numbersDescription: Across the measured years, a pattern becomes visible:
visualProof.conclusion: The satellite record supports the memory of earlier breakup, while leaving room for local causes and year-to-year variability.

newAbnormal.title: When "Normal" Shifts
newAbnormal.description: To make the shift legible, we compare the first four measured seasons with the later measured years.
newAbnormal.earlyPeriod: The blue line shows the 2017-2020 mean. The light band shows how much those years varied.
newAbnormal.livingOutside: The red line shows the 2021-2025 mean. In this comparison, the later years sit lower.
newAbnormal.consequence: is the estimate for this comparison window.
newAbnormal.result: That does not explain every local decision, but it does show that the physical season has shifted.

trajectory.title: A Direction, Not a Fate
trajectory.rate: The yearly record slopes downward. Treat the line as evidence of direction, not as a fixed date for the future.

future.trend: A shorter period of reliable ice changes the balance between ice roads, boats, harbor work, and safety decisions.
future.memory: For children in Uummannaq, ice is both inherited memory and a changing practical problem.
future.traditions: Dog teams, snowmobiles, boats, and local judgment now share a more unstable season.

toArctic.title: From Uummannaq to the Arctic
toArctic.description: Uummannaq shows one local shift. The Arctic-wide record gives the scale: winter maximums, summer minimums, old ice, and rapid regional warming.
toArctic.learnMoreTitle: Methodology & Data Sources – Arctic Sea-Ice Dashboard
toArctic.learnMoreContent:
The dashboard combines peer-reviewed datasets and refreshes them daily via an automated Python pipeline.

Data sources
NASA GISS – temperature anomalies: https://data.giss.nasa.gov/gistemp/
NOAA – daily Arctic sea-ice extent: https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/monitoring/seaice/
Our World in Data – global CO₂ emissions: https://ourworldindata.org/co2-and-greenhouse-gas-emissions

Processing steps
1. A GitHub Actions workflow triggers an update every night.
2. Latest CSV / JSON files are downloaded, validated and reshaped with Pandas.
3. Z-scores standardise temperature, ice extent and CO₂ to a common scale.
4. The cleaned dataset is written to data.json and served by FastAPI to the Next.js frontend.

seasonal.title: The Arctic Seasonal Record
seasonal.description: Each line traces one year of Arctic sea-ice extent, from autumn minimum to spring maximum and back again. Blue lines show older years, red lines show recent years.

early.title: 1979 - 2000: The Cold(er) Half
early.description: The first half of the record sits visibly higher. The ice had more room to recover through the year.

late.title: 2000 onward: The Recent Half
late.description: In the more recent years, the lines sit lower through much of the season.

current.title: Latest Available Year {{year}}
current.description: Finally, isolate {{year}} and compare it with the rest of the record.

breaking.title: Below the Earlier Range
breaking.description: Recent years often sit below the older envelope, especially around the winter maximum and late-summer minimum.

decades.title: Six Decades, One Direction
decades.description: Each colored band represents a decade of Arctic ice measurements. Watch them appear one by one and notice the unmistakable staircase downward. We open in the 1980s—the last decade before the losses dive below zero.

1980s.title: The 1980s: The Old Normal
1980s.description: This soft blue band is our starting point. Late-summer values still hovered around zero million km²—close to the historical average and a world many remember as 'normal.'

1990s.title: The 1990s: First Cracks
1990s.description: Each new decade settles slightly lower than the last. The changes seem gradual, but they're building momentum.

2000s.title: The 2000s: A Lower Baseline
2000s.description: For the first time, the curve spends much of the season below the older baseline. The shift is no longer subtle.

2010s.title: The 2010s: Acceleration
2010s.description: The decline steepens. Values around -0.6 million km² become common—territory that was unthinkable just decades earlier.

2020s.title: The 2020s: A Lower Range
2020s.description: The current decade sits near the bottom of the stack. The gap from the old baseline is especially clear late in the melt season.
2020s.acceleration: The data shows no short-term return to the older range.

bridge.decades: Each new decade stepped lower. But how did single years behave inside those stair-steps?

annual.title: Below the Average
annual.description: Each bar shows how much above (blue) or below (red) the long-term average that year finished. Think of zero as the "normal" line.
annual.analysis: The 1980s and 90s still moved around the average. From the 2000s onward, negative years become the rule rather than the exception.
annual.conclusion: The single-year view tells the same story: the baseline has moved lower.

drivers.intro: What is pushing the ice away? We have seen declining coverage and strong anomalies. Next, we put CO₂, global temperature, and Arctic temperature on the same scale.

connections.title: The Warming Signal
connections.description: Three lines, one story: atmospheric CO₂ (green), global temperature (blue), and Arctic temperature (red) have climbed for decades.
connections.causation: More greenhouse gases trap more heat. In the Arctic, that warming shows up especially strongly.
connections.conclusion: The Arctic is an early warning system. What happens there does not stay there.

zscore.title: Apples to Apples
zscore.description: How do you compare CO₂ levels (measured in parts per million) with temperature (degrees) and ice extent (square kilometers)?
zscore.method: By converting everything to the same scale: how many standard deviations each measurement is from its historical average. Now we can see the patterns clearly.

mirror.title: The Mirror Image
mirror.description: When we flip the ice-loss data upside down, the pattern becomes easier to compare: rising CO₂ and temperature move against falling ice.
mirror.conclusion: Three different measurements, one unified story. This is what climate change looks like in data.

2024.title: {{year}}: The Latest Comparison
2024.description: In the latest available year, {{year}}, the Arctic temperature anomaly sits well above the global mean.
2024.warning: The red bar is more than a number. It shows how unevenly warming is distributed.
2024.conclusion: What happens in the Arctic doesn't stay in the Arctic.

chatbot.greeting: Greetings, traveller! I'm Knud Rasmussen, Danish-Greenlandic explorer and collector of Inuit legends. Ask me anything about the Greenlandic folktales I recorded in Eskimo Folk-Tales. Behind the scenes, modern technology helps me recall these stories clearly, blending the spirit of my journeys with today's AI.
chatbot.error: An error occurred. The spirits have gone silent. Please try again later.
chatbot.description: This AI chatbot is trained on the 'Eskimo Folk-Tales', a unique collection of Greenlandic legends and myths gathered by Danish-Greenlandic polar explorer Knud Rasmussen (1879-1933) during his legendary Thule Expeditions.
chatbot.aboutKnud: Known as the 'Father of Eskimology,' Knud Rasmussen conducted groundbreaking expeditions across the Arctic from 1912-1924. Fluent in Greenlandic, he collected over 20,000 pages of Inuit oral traditions and stories of spirits, shamans, hunting, and Arctic life.
chatbot.folkTales: The 'Eskimo Folk-Tales' contain hundreds of stories: from Tulugaq the great raven who brought light to the world, to Sedna the sea goddess. These tales offer unique insights into the spiritual world of the Inuit.
chatbot.aiNote: Greetings, traveller. Ask me about specific legends, characters, or the meaning behind Arctic myths!

beta.title: Beta Preview
beta.description: This interactive Arctic story is still under development. You might encounter rough edges, visual hiccups or missing features while I smooth things out.
beta.feedback: I'd love your feedback on the way!
